coredata.dc:title: Polyaniline as an On−Off−On bright green fluorescent probe: Solvent directed synthesis, characterization and recognition of chromium through the inner filter effect
coredata.dc:creator: Pal, Sumana
Roy, Debiprasad
Bar, Nandagopal
Chowdhury, Shreyashi
Chowdhury, Pranesh
coredata.dc:description: Bright green fluorescent polyaniline (FPANI) has been synthesized to develop efficient inner filter effect pair (probe-anylate). The synthesized FPANI have dual emissive peaks (blue and green) depending on excitation wavelength. But the green fluorescence intensity is prominent and significantly bright having quantum yield in the order of 0.17. Solvent directs the quality of the probe (FPANI) in terms of molecular weight, physical appearance and optical properties. An analytical method has been developed to recognize trace level of chromium and lead simultaneously and selectively using On-Off-On characteristics of the synthesized FPANI. Cr(VI) switches ‘On’ to ‘Off’ mode of the probe due to inner filter effect, while Pb(II) brings back green fluorescence (‘On’ mode) from non-fluorescent one (‘Off’ mode) through quantitative chemical interaction with chromium.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green fluorescent oligomer
On-off-on chromium sensor
Inner filter effect

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing of metals through OFF-ON mechanism
Sensing of Cr(VI) through OFF mechanism
Sensing of Pb(II) through ON mechanism

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: CouncilÂ ofÂ Scientific and Industrial Research, India
Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors grateful for financial supports provided by the CSIR (No. 02(0331)/17/EMR-II dated November 8, 2017) & DST (Project No. EMR/2017/000963 ). The author sincerely thanks Prof. N. R. Jana for DLS measurements and MALDI-TOFF analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV-VIS spectrum of the FPANI synthesized in the water-ethanol mixture (volume ratio 1:0 and 1:1).
XRD pattern of the FPANI synthesized in the water-ethanol mixture (volume ratio 1:1).
FESEM images of bulk (A) and single particle (B) of FPANI synthesized in the water-ethanol mixture (volume ratio 1:1), DLS (B) and Zeta potential (C) of the same sample.

(A) Photoluminescence spectra of FPANI at different excitation wavelength, (B) blue emission (excited below 230 nm) and (C) green emission (excited above 230 nm) of the fluorescent PANI solution. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The effect of pH on the photoluminescence properties of FPANI.
(A) Quenching spectra of FPANI with varying concentration of Cr(VI) and (B) selectivity study of the FPANI at various 10−2 M metal ions.
(A) Absorption spectra of FPANI pure, Cr(VI) and FPANI-Cr(VI), (B) Fluorescence quenching mechanism of the FPANI synthesized in the water-ethanol mixture (volume ratio 1:1).
(A) Thirsting spectra of FPANI-Cr(VI) system with varying concentration of Pb(II) solution and (B) Selectivity study of the FPANI-Cr(VI) system towards Pb(II).
Solvent directed synthesis of fluorescent polyaniline (FPANI).

head.ce:title: Polyaniline as an On−Off−On bright green fluorescent probe: Solvent directed synthesis, characterization and recognition of chromium through the inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer & Nano Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Visva-Bharati University, Santiniketan, 731235, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Polymer & Nano Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Visva-Bharati University, Santiniketan, 731235, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bright green fluorescent polyaniline (FPANI) has been synthesized to develop efficient inner filter effect pair (probe-anylate). The synthesized FPANI have dual emissive peaks (blue and green) depending on excitation wavelength. But the green fluorescence intensity is prominent and significantly bright having quantum yield in the order of 0.17. Solvent directs the quality of the probe (FPANI) in terms of molecular weight, physical appearance and optical properties. An analytical method has been developed to recognize trace level of chromium and lead simultaneously and selectively using On-Off-On characteristics of the synthesized FPANI. Cr(VI) switches ‘On’ to ‘Off’ mode of the probe due to inner filter effect, while Pb(II) brings back green fluorescence (‘On’ mode) from non-fluorescent one (‘Off’ mode) through quantitative chemical interaction with chromium.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium usually exists in the environment as trivalent and hexavalent forms. The low dose Cr(III) is an essential nutrient for human because it plays a vital role in insulin action, and glucose, protein and lipid metabolism [1]. But, Cr(VI) is 500 times more hazardous than Cr(III) [2] due to its electronic configuration and oxidizing property. Cr(VI) is recognized as a human carcinogen, and may leads to cancers [3,4]. Ingestion through drinking water causes ulceration in gastrointestinal track, and severe damage to kidney and liver [3,4]. Inside the cell, Cr(VI) oxidizes vitamin C [2] and itself reduces to Cr(III), which forms stable complexes with nucleic acids and proteins. The Cr-DNA adduct formation are accountable for mutagenic smash up [5–8] through breaking in DNA strand. Therefore, the maximum allowable concentration of Cr(VI) in drinking water is set as 0.05 mg/L by World Health Organisation (WHO). Hence, it is essential to detect the trace level Cr(VI) from waste water before discharging sewage into main streams of water in our ecosystem [2].

The conventional analytical techniques for detection and estimation of Cr(VI) [9–11] from aqueous solution are electrochemical, surface plasmon field enhanced resonance light scattering, potentiometric, chromatograph, atomic absorption spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry, colorimetric, fluorimetric, etc. Each and every technique has some prospects and drawbacks [12–15]. As for example, fluorescence based sensing techniques possess a number of drawbacks such as low photo and chemical stability, uncontrollable excitation and emissions, blinking fluorescence, lower sensitivity and selectivity. Therefore, there is enough scope to improve the fluorimetric technique [16–18] through the development of new fluorescent material having On−Off−On property for selective and sensitive detection of Cr(VI).

Till date only very few materials have been reported for the sensing of Cr(VI). Broadly, this can be categories into three types which are (i) nano-composites [19–22] such as carbon nitride nanosheets, gold-graphene nanocomposites, furan-2-carbohydrazide, and polyaniline nanocomposite [23]. The last composite is highly sensitive and selective chemo-resistor based gas/vapor sensor. (ii) fluorescent carbon and graphene quantum dots [24–26] synthesized from diethylenetriamine, ethylenediamine, phytic acid, ammonium citrate and 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (iii) doped and grafted polymeric materials [27–29] such as diethyl 2-phthalimidomalonate (DPM) doped poly (vinyl chloride) membrane, polyaniline grafted graphene oxide, NiO/polyaniline p-p junction foam and xanthan gum grafted polyaniline [30]. But most of the synthetic procedure seems very complicated as it includes long reaction time, tedious multiple steps, high energy consumption and careful dialysis. Hence, the development of robust fluorescent polyanline is necessary to remove its synthetic complexity (doping or grafting) for the effective detection of Cr(VI). As per literature survey till date, there is no report of robust polyaniline based Cr(VI) sensor through inner filter effect.

Photo-physical processes [31,32] such as photo-induced electron transfer (PET), intra-molecular charge transfer (ICT), metal-ligand charge transfer (MLCT), electronic energy transfer (EET), and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) etc. are based on the intermolecular energy transfer between the chemo-sensor and analyte. A particular distance or a complementary geometry is required to fulfil the energy transfer conditions, which restricts their applications. Beside all these mechanisms, Inner Filter Effect (IFE) [33–35] has drawn much attention now a day. It is an important non-irradiative energy conversion strategy arises from the absorption of excitation (primary) and emission (secondary) of light by the quencher in the detecting systems. Since it does not require any direct link between the fluorophore and the quencher molecule, it needs not to fulfil the distance condition for energy transfer strictly. An efficient spectral overlap between the absorption band of the quencher, and excitation and emission band of the fluorophore is the prime requisite for the inner filter effect. Moreover, the process of sensing by IFE is very simple, flexible, significantly sensitive and selective. Bright blue fluorescent polyaniline (PANI) microspheres with a high quantum yield have been reported [36]. However, there is no report regarding green fluorescent PANI so far. Green fluorescent PANI is expected to exhibit IFE in the presence of Cr(VI). Thus the designed synthesis of later PANI gains immense importance today. Green fluorescence has strong biological relevance because green fluorescent protein (GFP) acts as a marker for gene expression. Scientists Roger Y. Tsien, Osamu Shimomura and Martin Chalfie were awarded the Nobel Prize in Chemistry on 2008 for their discovery and development of the GFP [37,38].

In the present study, bright green fluorescent probe based on polyaniline has been synthesized primarily for selective recognition of trace level chromium and searched for secondary application of the resultant complex to detect lead ion. An attempt has been made to understand the role of solvent in controlling fluorescent polyaniline synthesis and investigation of sensing mechanism for selective recognition.

Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using KBr pellets by Shimadzu-8400S spectrometer in the range of 400-4000 cm−1. The NMR spectra of the samples were recorded in ppm by using 400.152 MHz BRUKER NMR spectrometer in D2O containing a small amount of TMS as internal standard. The NMR data reported in terms of chemical shift and multiplicity (s: singlet, d: doublet, t: triplet, m: multiplet). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded at room temperature by Shimadzu-UV-3101PC spectrometer in the range of 200–900 nm. The thermal stability of the polymer was measured by Pyris Diamond TG/DTA (PerkinElmer, STA-6000) thermal analyzer in nitrogen atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The fluorescence spectrum was recorded using a PerkinElmer (LS55) Luminescence spectrometer. The molecular weight of the polymer was determined by MALDI-TOF Analyzer (Applied Bio-system) using α-cyano-4-hydroxy-cinnamic acid and trifuloroacetic acid matrix. Dynamic Light Scattering Study (DLS) and Zeta potential of the samples were measured by using Malvern Zeta-sizer Ver.6.34 instrument. XRD was carried out by Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer equipped with a conventional Cu Kα X-ray radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) source. High resolution micro structural analysis was performed by Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM SUPRA 35VP). All the graphs are plotted using the software origin 8.6.

The analytical-grade aniline (ANI), ammonium persulfate (APS), ethanol, magnesium chloride, led nitrate, cadmium nitrate, copper sulphate, and potassium dichromate were obtained from Merck, India. The other reagents were used as received without further purification. Double-distilled water was used throughout the experiment to prepare the solutions.

The synthesis of bright green fluorescent polyaniline (FPANI) was performed through robust oxidative pathway. In a 50 mL round bottom flask, fixed amount of aniline monomer was added into 30 mL of suitable solvents (Table 1 ). After complete mixing, varying amount of ammonium persulfate was added to initiate oxidative polymerization. The optimized ANI:APS mole ratio was found to be 4:5 (Table S1). The reaction mixture was kept under vigorous stirring maintaining the optimized conditions (pH, time and temperature were 4.15, 8 h and 30 °C respectively). The mixture turned into specific colour indicating completion of the reaction. Various types of solvents as mentioned in the table were applied to obtain the optimized solvent ratio. The water-ethanol mixture (1:1 vol ratio) produced the desired quality polymer that exhibited the bright green fluorescence under a UV lamp at 317 nm with quantum yield of 17%. When pure water was used as the solvent, dark green precipitate of bulk polyaniline was found. The gradual increase of ethanol proportion (item number 1–6) checked the precipitation and produced various solutions having characteristic colour and spectral properties. The water-ethanol mixture (1:1 vol ratio) yielded wine red solution with no precipitation. Replacing ethanol by THF (item no 7), no appreciable changes was noticed. However, use of propanol and methanol in place of ethanol (items no 8–9) generated brown precipitate and yellowish solution respectively. Thus solvent directed (controlled) the synthesis in terms of physical appearance and optical property.

Different concentration of Cr(VI) solutions was prepared using double distilled water. Then 30 μL of FPANI was added to 3 mL Cr(VI) solutions of various concentrations and the mixture was incubated for 15 min to reach the steady state with respect to optical activity. After that emission spectra were recorded.

The FPANI was directly synthesized from aniline in environmentally benign mixed solvents (alcohol and water in different volume ratio) using APS as oxidant. In acidic aqueous medium, dark green precipitation of PANI was noticed due to high degree of polymerization (bulk polymerization) (Scheme 1 ). However, unusual characteristics were observed in mixed solvents. It was found that with increasing the proportion of alcohol, colour of the solution became red wine gradually with no precipitation indicating formation of colloidal polymer instead of bulk one. The complete replacement of water by alcohol produced dark wine red solution. Probably low degree of polymerization (oligomerization) favoured the formation of colloidal polymer having wine red colour appearance. Thus solvent played a vital role in controlling the degree of polymerization, which ultimately produced bright green fluorescent polyaniline. Formation of oligomer (containing 3–4 repeating units) was confirmed by MALDI-TOFF analysis (Fig. S5).

Polymerization of aniline occurred though anilinium ion formation [39] and para polymerization. The mineral acids added from outside generally supply the required proton to produce anilinium ion. However, in the present case the internally produced proton (through hydrolysis of ammonium salts, Scheme 1) generated anilinium ion. When the medium was purely aqueous, higher degree of hydrolysis supplied sufficient proton, which induced high degree of polymerization (DP). In the case of water alcohol mixture, controlled hydrolysis occurred. As a result, the polymerization occurred through longer induction period, which was responsible for the formation of polyaniline oligomer of intermediate DP. Complete replacement of water by alcohol restricted the hydrolysis which in turn result low DP.

The stability of oligomers of different sizes may be explained on the basis of cage formation theory as put forwarded by Stejskal et al. [40]. The strong hydrogen bond between ethanol and water played a crucial role in the formation of polyaniline microstructure. The strong bond produced suitable cage for the formation micro-sized polyaniline [41]. Thus ethanol water mixture wrapped the PANI chains and prevented further aggregation during the formation of micro PANI. Replacement of ethanol by other solvents such as methanol, propanol, THF, etc changed the pH of the medium and size of the hydrogen bonded cage drastically. As a result desired micro polyaniline was not obtained.

The synthesis of green fluorescent polymer was performed by a single step polymerization method. The formation of the polyaniline was confirmed by the FTIR, NMR, UV–Vis, DLS, molecular weight measurement, zeta potential and thermal analysis.

FTIR spectrum: The FTIR spectrum of FPANI (Fig. S6) showed characteristic peaks at 617, 746, 874, 1131, 1403, 1493, 1559, 2354, 2588, 2829 and 3154 cm−1. The small band at 874 and 2829 cm−1 signifies the presence of aromatic C–H unit [42]. The sharp bands at 1493 and 1559 cm−1 (νC
C) appeared due to CC bond vibrations of the benzenoid and quinoied rings [43,44] respectively. The small band at 2354 cm−1 probably appeared due to the CO2 absorption by the polymeric material. The sharp and intense band at 113 cm−1 was due to in-plane deformation of quinoid ring. The later band coincides with the in-plane deformation of C–H band [45]. The IR bands characteristic to the C–H bond vibrations are represented by the peaks 617, 746, 874, 1403, 2829 cm−1 [46]. The broad stretching at 3154 cm−1 revealed the presence of secondary N–H bond [36]. Hence, benzeniod and quinoid rings along with secondary amine group confirmed the polymerization of aniline (Scheme 1).

UV–Vis spectra: Two characteristics absorption peaks of different intensity occurred at 230 and 280 nm in the UV-VIS spectra (Fig. 1 ) of the synthesized water soluble FPANI. The strong absorbance at 230 nm attributed to π-π* transition of the benzenoid moiety and weak absorbance at 280 nm occurred due to the polaron-π* electronic transitions [47]. The occurrences of polarons were due to charge transfer from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) under photo excitations [48]. The nature as well as the position of the bands confirmed the formation of the FPANI. It was noticed that the intensity of the absorption band changed remarkably with the water:ethanol ratio. Hyperchromic shift (i.e. increases of absorption intensity) observed with the gradual increase of ethanol proportion (less polar solvent). This implied that spectral shifts were sensitive to the solvent polarity.

NMR analysis: The 1H NMR spectrum of the synthesized FPANI was measured (Fig. S7) for the structural investigation. The spectrum contains two major peaks at δ: 6.5 (b) and 7.0 (a) due to the aromatic ring protons on phenylene (benzenoid) and a di-substituted phenylene unit (quinoid) respectively [49]. The multiplet at δ = 5.6–6.0 probably indicated the protons attached to benzenoid nitrogen (H–N). Another broad peak located at δ = 7.5 may be attributed to the water protons bound by the –NH groups through hydrogen bonding [35]. Hence, the 1H NMR data supported the proposed structure of FPANI (Scheme 1).

Thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA): The TGA traces of the synthesized polymer (FPANI) within the temperature range (40–900 °C) are presented in Fig. S8. It was observed that slight weight loss (0.6663%) occurred up to 100 °C, which may be due to the loss of moisture. The depolymerisation of the polymer started at 150 °C and continued up to 200 °C. Thus the synthesized polymer was stable up to 150 °C and it may be used safely within this temperature.

XRD studies: The X-ray Diffraction (XRD) study of the synthesized polymer was elucidated in Fig. 2 . The XRD trace shows three major sharp peaks at 2ϴ values of 16.85, 23.75 and 34° with varying intensity along with two minor peaks at 11.92 and 35.87°. The sharp peaks indicated the crystalline nature of the synthesized polymer. The crystallinity originated probably due to the presence of benzenoid and quinoid rings in polymer chain [50]. The intensity of the XRD peak generally represents the crystallite population at different planes. The sharp and intense peak at 16.85° suggested maximum population at (121) crystal plane with corresponding d-spacing 5.25 Å as calculated form Debye Scherer equation [51]. The inter planner distances (d-spacing) 3.74 Å and 2.63 Å corresponding the peaks at 23.75 and 34° were very close to the crystalline plane of (113) and (332) respectively [51]. Therefore, the high quality crystalline polymer was synthesized successfully.

The morphological analysis of the synthesized FPANI was performed with the help of field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (Fig. 3 A). The micrographs of the polymer reflected the heterogeneous dispersion of the polymeric micro-particle containing different shape of molecules. Major proportion of the molecules was found in oval shape (Fig. 3B) with the minor axis of 1.02 ± 0.02 μm. DLS (Fig. 3C) data also suggested the same results. The zeta potential (Fig. 3D) was found to be +3.66 mV, which implied that the surface of the polymeric particle was positively charged.

The fluorescence quantum yield (ϕ
f ) measurement of green FPANI was carried out at 296K in aqueous medium by taking 1-methylindole (in n-Hexane medium, refractive index = 1.426) as a reference material by eq. (1) [52].

)

Where, ϕ
f and ϕ
f
R represent the fluorescence quantum yield of green FPANI and reference material respectively. The terms І
f , A and n designated as intensity, absorbance and refractive index respectively for the solvent of the polymer FPANI. Similarly the terms І
f
R
, AR and nR correspond to the fluorescence intensity, absorbance and refractive index respectively for the solvent of the reference material. The measurement was performed at λ = 317 nm (fluorescence excitation wavelength) maintaining very low (<0.05) absorbance for sample and reference materials to determine the І
f and І
f
R respectively. The obtained value of ϕ
f for FPANI in water was found to be 17%. The quantum yield indicated that the polymer could be used as a potential fluorophore.

At various excitation: The emission spectra of FPANI (synthesized in the water-ethanol mixture at volume ratio 1:1) were carried out under various excitation wavelengths from 200 to 330 nm. It is evident that the synthesized fluorophore exhibited dual emissive peaks which were predominating on both sides of critical excitation wavelength 230 nm (Fig. 4 A). The emission spectra localized in two main domains namely: (i) blue fluorescence (Fig. 4B) (λem-max = 343 nm, excited below 230 nm) and (ii) green fluorescence (Fig. 4C) (λem-max = 400 nm, excited above 230 nm). The fluorescence intensity in the later domain was significantly more than the former one. Thus the band located under λem-max = 400 nm may be considered as major i.e. maximum number of molecules were fluorescence active in this particular excitation. Thus the inspection of emission revealed that the synthesized materials could be considered as bright green fluorophore. The exhibition of dual fluorescence probably originated due to the existence of two energy populations at the excited state.

At various solvents: Emission spectra of PANI synthesized under various water-ethanol ratios is shown in Fig. S3. Fluorescence intensity increased marginally with the gradual replacement of water by ethanol, became maxima at 1:1 vol ratio, and then decreased with the further increase of ethanol content. The degree of polymerization (DP) decreased with the increase of alcohol proportion. The DP was found 3–5 at 1:1 vol ratio, which exhibited maxima in the fluorescence intensity curve. The fluorescence property was seriously affected beyond the specific range (3–5). Thus, solvent played a major role in controlling green fluorescence. Emission spectra of PANI synthesized under various solvents (Fig. S4.) also revealed that water-ethanol (1:1) system performed the best.

At various pH: The effect of pH on the photoluminescence properties of FPANI was determined at the λex = 317 nm. The normalized results (Fig. 5 ) exhibited that there is a prominent red shift as the pH of the medium decreased. With the decrease of pH (i.e. increase in H+ concentration), more number of polyanilines were protonated and as a result the energy gap between ground state (S0) and excited state (S1) (in the light of Jablonski diagram) probably diminished. Consequently a red shift was observed. The same conclusion may be drawn from the un-normalized results (Fig. S9) at different pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of metals through OFF-ON mechanism
Sensing of Cr(VI) through OFF mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Sensing of Cr(VI) was carried out using green fluorescence property of the synthesized material and its quenching (OFF) behaviour. The spectra were measured using aqueous solution of the FPANI (Fig. 6 A). The PL intensity of the polymer solution was decreased marginally with gradual increase of Cr(VI) concentration. It is evident that the FPANI showed detectable fluorescence quenching in a solution containing Cr(VI) as low as 10−8 M. Thus the limit of detection (LOD) of the present system was 10−8 M. Hence, the FPANI-Cr(VI) system may be described as “fluorescence off” system. Considerable electrostatic interaction between polyaniline chain and dichromate chain is possibly responsible for this type of specific quenching. Moreover, Stern Volmer (SV) plot [Fig. S10] had been chosen for the better understanding of quenching phenomenon.

In order to examine the detection specificity of this chromium sensor, fluorescence measurements were carried out by incubating the FPANI with different types of 10−2 M metal ions, namely Cd(II), Cu(II), Hg(II), Pb(II), Cr(III) and Cr(VI) as shown in Fig. 6B. Significant quenching of photoluminescence was observed only in case of Cr(VI). Thus the developed green FPANI specifically recognized Cr(VI). The involved quenching and specific recognition may be explained in the light of inner filter effect (IFE).

Inner filter effect: The mechanistic pathway for ON to OFF could be explained on the basis of the inner filter effect (IFE). IFE may be defined as non-irradiative energy conversion strategy arises from the absorption of excitation (primary) and emission of light (secondary) by the quencher. Hence, it is important to choose a suitable absorber (anylate) and fluorophore pair for the IFE-based fluorescent probe. The absorption spectra of FPANI, Cr(VI) and FPANI-Cr(VI) are shown in Fig. 7 A. Both the materials FPANI and Cr(VI) exhibited two absorption bands while FPANI-Cr(VI) surprisingly showed three bands. The position of bands were as follows: 230 and 280 nm for FPANI with low intensity, 275 and 350 nm for Cr(VI) with comparatively high intensity and 230, 280 and 350 nm for FPANI-Cr(VI) with intermediate intensity. Appearance of the new broad peak at λ max = 350 nm in FPANI-Cr(VI) indicated the incorporation of Cr(VI) into the polymeric moiety.

The fluorescence spectrum of FPANI (Fig. 7B) revealed that the polymer showed λ max = 400 nm upon excitation at λex = 317 nm. It is evident that the absorption spectra of Cr(VI) overlapped with both the excitation (marked as red) and emission spectra (marked as blue) of the FPANI-Cr(VI) system. The phenomena fulfilled the criteria of inner filter effect. Therefore, Cr(VI) and FPANI acted as a good IFE pair.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of Pb(II) through ON mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The FPANI-Cr(VI) (fluorescence off system) showed fluorescence on in presence of trace amount of lead ion (Fig. 8 A). It was found that at 10−2 M of Pb(II), the fluorescence intensity of FPANI-Cr(VI)–Pb(II) system reached almost equivalent with the fluorescence spectrum of original polymer, FPANI. The minimum concentration of Pb(II) required to regenerate (fluorescence ON) traceable fluorescence was 10−8 M (limit of detection). Lead ion possesses strong affinity towards chromate, which ultimately formed very stable lead chromate [53]. Thus the Pb(II) executed an important role in removing Cr(VI) from polymer moiety. The removal of chromate from the vicinity of PANI regenerated the original fluorescence property of the later. Lead chromate formation successfully explained the “OFF” to “ON” mechanism.

To study the selectivity of Pb(II) sensor, fluorescence measurements were performed by leaching the FPANI-Cr(VI) system with different types of 10−2 M metal ions, such as Cd(II), Pb(II), Al(III), Mg(II) and Zn(II) (Fig. 8B). There occurred considerable thirsting (ON) of fluorescence spectrum only in case of Pb(II) ion. Thus FPANI-Cr(VI) system may be treated as a selective sensor for Pb(II).

ce:list-item.ce:label: (i)
ce:list-item.ce:para: Nonconventional fluorescent polyaniline (dual emissive: green and blue) having desired shape (oval), size (minor axis of 1.02 ± 0.20 μm) and crystalline nature is synthesized by a robust oxidative pathway. Green emission (while excited above 230 nm) was dominant compared to blue emission (while excited below 230 nm). The synthesized PANI exhibited bright green fluorescence under a UV lamp at 317 nm with quantum yield of 17%. Dual fluorescence originated due to the existence of two energy populations at the excited state. The polymer exhibited prominent red shift as the pH of the medium decreased.

ce:list-item.ce:label: (ii)
ce:list-item.ce:para: The solvent directed (controlled) synthetic procedure was optimized thoroughly and the best results were obtained at: Alcohol:Water = 1:1 (by volume), Aniline monomer: APS initiator = 4:5 (mole ratio), temperature (30 °C), pH (4.15) and time (8 h). Gradual replacement of water by alcohol directs the system to produce polyaniline oligomer having designed spectral properties (green fluorescence) relevant to analytical utility.

ce:list-item.ce:label: (iii)
ce:list-item.ce:para: The synthesized FPANI and FPANI-Cr(VI) systems may be treated as an efficient sensors for Cr(VI) and Pb(II) respectively. The developed ON-OFF-ON probe system could detect trace amount of Cr(VI) and Pb(II) (limit of detection = 10−8 in both cases) sensibly and selectively.

ce:list-item.ce:label: (iv)
ce:list-item.ce:para: It is understood that Cr(VI) led inner filter effect is mainly responsible for switching ‘On’ to ‘Off’ mode of the synthesized polyaniline probe, while Pb(II) brings back fluorescence property (‘On’ mode) from ‘Off’ situation due to it selective and quantitative reactivity towards chromate (i.e. Lead chromate formation ability). The FPANI (probe) and Cr(VI) (anylate) emerges as a good IFE pair.

ce:section.ce:para: Sumana Pal: Methodology, Software, Writing - original draft. Debiprasad Roy: Data curation, Writing - original draft, Software. Nandagopal Bar: Writing - review & editing. Shreyashi Chowdhury: Visualization, Investigation. Pranesh Chowdhury: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing - original draft.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors grateful for financial supports provided by the CSIR (No.02(0331)/17/EMR-II dated November 8, 2017) & DST (Project No. EMR/2017/000963). The author sincerely thanks Prof. N. R. Jana for DLS measurements and MALDI-TOFF analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

Optimization results, solvent dependent absorption and emission spectra, MALDI-TOFF, FTIR, NMR, TGA, pH dependent emission spectra and SV plot are given in Supplementary information.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.polymer.2020.122292.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Removal of chromium(VI) from aqueous solutions using polyaniline doped with sulfuric acid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Zhang, H. Ma, B. Wang, Removal of Chromium(VI) from Aqueous Solutions Using Polyaniline Doped with Sulfuric Acid, Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 49 (2010) 9998-10004. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1021/ie1008794.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. K. Mondal, D. Roy, P. Chowdhury, Designed functionalization of reduced graphene oxide for sorption of Cr(VI) over a wide pH range: a theoretical and experimental perspective, New J. Chem. 42 (2018) 16960-16971. DOI: 10.1039/C8NJ03794A
